---
title: "[Cities as Inverted Watersheds]{.pink_font}"
author: 
- Sanghoon Lee - UBC Sauder
- Seung Hoon Lee - Yonsei Economics
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: slides.css
    chalkboard: true
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    margin: 0.05
    self-contained: false
    highlight-style: github
    incremental: false
    slide-number: true
    center: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE, comment = NA)
options(tibble.print_max = Inf)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(htmltools)
library(leaflet)
library(sf)
library(terra)
library(tmap)
tmap_mode("view")
library(scales)
library(here)
library(gt)
here::i_am("Slides-2026_04_ualberta.qmd")
```


**Geographic boundaries are important!**

:::{.fragment}
- No boundaries, no data.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- The way we draw boundaries can affect our understanding of the world (e.g., MAUP, Gerrymandering)
:::

<br />

:::{.fragment}

City and subcentre boundaries

:::{.fragment}
- Official city boundaries (e.g., CBSAs in the US, CMAs in Canada) do not exist for many countries (e.g. China, India, Korea)
    - Even when they exist, they are not always comparable across countries and over time.
:::
:::{.fragment}
- Subcentre boundaries do not exist for *any* country, making it difficult to study them.
:::

:::

---

Literature has emerged on delineating clusters from granular data

:::{.fragment}

- City boundaries - Rozenfeld et al. (2011), Duranton (2015), Rappaport (2025) 

- Subcenters - McMillen (2001), Redfearn (2007)

- Ethnic enclaves - Dai and Schiff (2023)

- JUE special issue (2021) - de Bellefon et al, Dingle et al, Ch et al, Arribas-Bel et al, Baragwanath et al, Bosker et al, Moreno-Monroy et al, Galdo et al, Henderson et al, Dijkstra et al.

:::

<br />

:::{.fragment}
Many challenges still remain. (To be discussed)
:::

---

## [[Overview of the Paper]{.white_font}]{text-align="center"} {background-color="#FBBC13" .center}



---

Propose a new method to delineate cities and subcentres

::: {.fragment}
- **Mountains** on a **smoothed** density surface.
:::
::: {.fragment}
- **Watershed segmentation algorithm** to delineate the mountains.
:::
::: {.fragment}
- **Smoothing parameter ($\sigma$)** controls the granularity: low $\sigma$ for subcentres, high $\sigma$ for cities.
:::

---

##  {background-image="figures/slide_intro_maps_1.png" background-size="cover"}

---

##  {background-image="figures/slide_intro_maps_2.png" background-size="cover"}

---

##  {background-image="figures/slide_intro_maps_3.png" background-size="cover"}

---

##  {background-image="figures/slide_intro_maps_4.png" background-size="cover"}

---

##  {background-image="figures/slide_intro_maps_5.png" background-size="cover"}

---

##  {background-image="figures/slide_intro_maps_6.png" background-size="cover"}

---

##  {background-image="figures/slide_intro_maps_7.png" background-size="cover"}

---

##  {background-image="figures/slide_intro_maps_8.png" background-size="cover"}

---


##  {background-image="figures/slide_intro_maps_9.png" background-size="cover"}

---

##  {background-image="figures/slide_intro_maps_10.png" background-size="cover"}

---

**New** boundaries for

:::{.fragment}
- **Subcentres** for [the entire continental US](https://subcenters.sanghoon.ca/) and for [Edmonton](edmonton_c100_s5.html)
:::

:::{.fragment}
- All **cities** from [1975 to 2020 in the world](https://watersheds.sanghoon.ca/)
:::

---

### Comparison against previous methods

:::{.fragment}
- Theoretically grounded in the monocentric city model
- Our method produces similar results as the commuting-flow-based approach, without requiring commuting-flow data.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Use the same algorithm to identify clusters at different levels (e.g. entire cities or subcenters within cities) with a single parameter adjustment.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Our method requires only one global parameter, the smoothing parameter $\sigma$, to identify clusters at different levels, allowing large-scale applications.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Our method outperforms the density threshold method (the prevailing method) in terms of how well they can match the CBSA boundaries.
:::

---

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="75%", fig.align='center'}
#generated by "tasks/calc_jaccard_index/jaccard_index_density_based4_dev_analysis.R"
include_graphics("figures/scatter_ws_density.png")
```

---

### New facts from the new data

US cities and subcentres

:::{.fragment}
- City size distribution follows lognormal distribution, rather than Zipf's law.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- The central place theory is missing an important channel - land requirement
:::

:::{.fragment}
- The spatial distribution of economic activities across subcentres differs substantially from that across cities. 
  - e.g., drinking places vs. restaurants
  - Negative urban wage premium across subcentres.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- The spatial distribution of economic activities among very small cities (pop < 10,000) differs substantially from that among large cities.
  - Negative urban wage premium across small cities.
:::

---

All cities in the world from 1975 to 2020

:::{.fragment}
- World city size distribution follows lognormal distribution, rather than Zipf's law.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Urbanization patterns from 1975 to 2020

  :::{.fragment}
  - Global population growth was driven primarily by the expansion of existing cities, rather than the creation of new ones.
  :::
  :::{.fragment}
  - Gibrat's law holds for all cities except for the lower tail of the distribution.
  :::
  :::{.fragment}
  - Median person lived in a city with population 0.66M in 1975, which increased to 1.42M by 2020 (115% increase)
  - 90th quantile person lived in a city with population 4.3M in 1975, which increased to 11.7M by 2020 (172% incresae)
  :::
:::

---

## [[Previous methods]{.white_font}]{text-align="center"} {background-color="#FBBC13" .center}

---

### Commuting-Flow-based approach

Group contiguous areas that have high level of interactions with an urban core, measured by commuting flows.

:::{.fragment}
- Official city boundaries: CBSAs, CMAs, ...
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Duranton (2015)
:::

:::{.fragment}

#### Challenge

- Commuting flow data are relatively difficult to obtain.

:::

---

### Density based approaches

Set a density threshold on a smoothed density surface and find contiguous areas above the threshold.

<br />

:::{.fragment}
Prevaling approach. Encompasses various methods:

- grid-based approach
- DBSCAN
- city clustering algorithm
:::

:::{.fragment}
Main challenges

- Need for local thresholds: Different areas often require different thresholds to separate clusters.
- Arbitrary selection of density thresholds.
:::

---

```{r echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/example.png")
```

---

### Not possible to separately identify all three clusters with a single threshold.

```{r echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/example_hor.png")
```

---

### This can happen both within a city and across cities.

Need to choose the threshold parameters locally, using the "local knowledge".

:::{.fragment}
- Different thresholds across areas make it difficult to compare the identified clusters.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Arbitrariness in cluster size and characteristics.
:::

---

### Core based approaches

Subcenter delineation: McMillen (2001), Redfearn (2007)

Start from local maxima and can identify all the peaks without adjusting any parameter 

```{r echo=FALSE, out.width="75%", fig.align='center'}
include_graphics("figures/example_with_max.png")
```

:::{.fragment}
Challenge: Not trivial how to set the boundaries of each cluster on *2D* surface.
:::

---

## [[Cities as Inverted Watersheds]{.white_font}]{text-align="center"} {background-color="#FBBC13" .center}

---

### Watershed - the area of land that flows to a common outlet (i.e., a [pour point]{.pink_font}) and forms a basin.

```{r echo=FALSE, out.width="45%", fig.align='center'}
# include_graphics("https://streamline.imgix.net/d0137d76-8f43-408c-9b43-aa3e54f62403/dd342d5f-7adf-4256-9935-7d4b22d7db23/Watershed%20diagram.jpg?ixlib=rb-1.1.0&w=2000&h=2000&fit=max&or=0&s=c559560279d62ca63db72008d5950fdc")
include_graphics("figures/Watershed_diagram.jpeg")
```
::: {style="text-align: right;"}
[Source: https://www.rcrcd.org/what-is-a-watershed]{.tiny}
:::

:::{.fragment}  
Efficient algorithms and tools available to identify watersheds from GIS raster data.
:::

---

### Mountains become watershed basins when inverted!

```{r echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/example_watershed.png")
```

:::{.fragment}  
Works well on a 2D surface. Imagine placing a water drop on each pixel and let it flow down.
:::

---

Given a smoothed density surface, the algorithm uniquely identifies all the peaks and boundaries. No parameters to adjust in this step.

<br />

::: {.fragment}
The only parameter throughout the whole process is the smoothing parameter $\sigma$ to create the smoothed density surface.

Adjusting $\sigma$ allows us to delineate clusters at varying scales: larger values for entire cities and smaller values for subcenters within cities.
:::

---

###  {vertical-align="middle"}

```{r echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/example_diff_sigma.png")
```

---

### Smoothing

Two approaches to choosing the smoothing parameter

:::{.fragment}
- Statistics: find the single $\sigma$ that best estimates the true density function.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Computer Vision: different features reveal themselves at different $\sigma$. (e.g., leaves with low $\sigma$ and tree with high $\sigma$)
:::

:::{.fragment}
We follow the computer vision approach: subcenters with low $\sigma$ and cities with high $\sigma$.
:::

---

### The Structure of Images - Koenderink (1984)

:::{.fragment}
Foundational paper in computer vision
:::

:::{.fragment}
Causality in scale space

- No "Hallucinations": **Gaussian smoothing** is the only method that guarantees no new peaks or fake details are created during blurring.

- One-Way Simplification: Features can only merge or flatten out as scale increases, ensuring every coarse shape has a clear "ancestor" in the original data.

- Scale Hierarchy: This creates a tree-like structure where a single feature at a large scale "branches" into multiple finer details as you move toward smaller scales.
:::

:::{.fragment}
Our delineation preserves the topological hierarchy: a cluster at higher $\sigma$ (e.g., cities) can be broken down into clusters at lower $\sigma$ (e.g., subcenters).
:::

:::{.fragment}
[Online demo](https://koenderink.streamlit.app/)
:::

---

### Theoretical Grounding

:::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
<br/><br/>
```{r echo=FALSE, out.width='100%'}
include_graphics("figures/example.png")
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

- A variation of the **monocentric city model** with three city centres at A, B, and C and workers choose where to live and work and how much land to consume.

:::{.fragment}
- Commuting flows follow the direction of increasing density.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- The watershed boundaries coincide with the flow-based approach, but do not require commuting-flow data.
:::
:::
::::::

---

#### Summary

:::{.fragment}
- Monocentric city model. Produces similar results as the flow-based approach, without requiring commuting-flow data.
:::
:::{.fragment}
- Use the same algorithm to identify clusters at different levels (e.g. entire cities or subcenters within cities) with a single parameter adjustment. 
:::
:::{.fragment}
- Topological hierarchy of clusters at different scales.
:::
:::{.fragment}
- No need to handpick parameters for each region, allowing large-scale applications.
:::
:::{.fragment}
- Applicable to various data types (e.g., point, polygon, raster), as long as we can generate a density surface.
:::

---


## [[Delineating Subcentres in the US]{.white_font}]{text-align="center"} {background-color="#FBBC13" .center}

---

### Safegraph data

:::{.fragment}
- POIs (Points of Interest) for the whole continental US
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Visited in 2019
:::

:::{.fragment}
- 4.6 million places with lon and lat.
:::

<!-- 
Canada
- Major cities (Toronto, Montreal, Vancouver) at this point.
- All POIs.
-->

:::{.fragment}
- Exclude the POIs with the NAICS codes starting with 11 (Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing and Hunting), 21 (Mining, Quarrying, and Oil and Gas Extraction), 22 (Utilities), 31-33 (Manufacturing), 55 (Management of Companies and Enterprises).
:::

---

::: {.scroll-output .small}
```{r echo=FALSE}
# temp <- read_rds("../data/pois_visited_2019.rds") %>% filter(!str_extract(naics_code, "^\\d{2}") %in% c("11", "21", "22", "31", "32", "33", "55"))
# atlanta_boundry <- st_read("../data/cont_us/cbsa_boundaries.shp") %>% filter(NAME %>% str_detect("Atlanta"))
# temp %>% st_intersection(atlanta_boundry) %>% write_rds("pois_presentation.rds")
pois_sfm <- read_rds("tables/pois_presentation.rds")

set.seed(123)
sgraph_sample <- pois_sfm %>% select(`POI Name`=location_name, `NAICS`=naics_code) %>% sample_n(100) %>%
  st_transform(crs=4326) %>% 
  mutate(Longitude=st_coordinates(.)[,1], Latitude=st_coordinates(.)[,2] ) %>% 
  st_drop_geometry() 

sgraph_sample %>%   knitr::kable()
```
:::

---

```{r echo=F}
include_graphics("figures/pois_atlanta.png")
```

---

## [[Processing steps]{.pink_font}]{text-align="center"} {.center}

---

### Convert POI data to a raster data.

- Project the POI data to [USA Contiguous Albers Equal Area Conic, USGS (EPSG = 5070)]{.pink_font}.
  - Preserve area size.
  - Use meters as distance unit.
- Create a raster data with a 100 meter by 100 meter resolution, covering the whole continental US.
- Count the number of POIs for each cell.

---

:::: {.columns style="display: flex; align-items: center;"}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![POIs (Atlanta downtown)](figures/tm_pois_atl.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Raster](figures/tm_raster_atl.png)
:::

::::

---

### Smoothing the Surface

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
We apply the Gaussian filter to the raster data.

[Two dimensional moving average]{.pink_font} with the Gaussian kernel as weights.

$$g(x,y) = \frac{1}{2 \pi \sigma^2} \exp^{-(x^2+y^2)/(2\sigma^2)}$$

x and y are the distances from the origin in the horizontal and the vertical axes.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r echo=FALSE, out.width='85%'}
include_graphics("figures/gaussian_figure.png")
```
:::

::::

Koenderink (1984) shows that Gaussian smoothing is the only method that guarantees no new peaks or fake details are created during blurring.

<!-- We use 5 pixels (i.e., 500 meters) for $\sigma$ as the baseline: the cells that are 500, 1000, and 1,500 meters away get 61%, 14%, and 1% weights compared to the own cell. -->

---

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Raster](figures/tm_raster_atl_large_nobox.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![After Gaussian smoothing](figures/tm_raster_atl_gaussian.png)
:::

::::

---

### Invert the smoothed surface

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Pre-inverted surface](figures/tm_raster_atl_gaussian.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Inverted surface](figures/inv_atl.png)
:::

::::

---

### Create D8 flow pointer

We use D8 flow pointer tool to identify the pour points and their watershed boundaries.

```{r echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.align='center'}
include_graphics("figures/d8_flow_algorithm.png")
```

---


### Identifying peaks

Pour points of the inverted smoothed surface.

Local minima of the inverted smoothed surface, but not local maxima.
- The D8 flow pointer of the [inverted]{.pink_font} surface is 0.
- The D8 flow pointer of the [pre-inverted]{.pink_font} surface is [not]{.pink_font} 0, to exclude flat areas.

---

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Inverted surface](figures/inv_atl.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Pour points (peaks)](figures/inv_atl_peak.png)
:::

::::

---

### Identify watersheds

For each pour point, identify the watershed cells flowing to the pour point using the D8 flow pointer.

---

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Pour points (peaks)](figures/inv_atl_peak.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Watersheds](figures/watersheds_atl.png)
:::

::::

---

### Count the number of POIs for each watershed.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Watersheds](figures/watersheds_atl.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Watersheds with N POIs](figures/watersheds_pois_atl.png)
:::

::::

---

#### Final output with $\sigma$ = 500 meters (baseline)

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Atlanta](figures/atl_ours_5.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![New York](figures/ny_ours_5.png)
:::

::::

---

#### Final output with $\sigma$ = 800 meters

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Atlanta](figures/atl_ours_8.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![New York](figures/ny_ours_8.png)
:::

::::

---

### Final outputs for Continental US Subcentres

[Continental US](https://subcenters.sanghoon.ca/)

[Edmonton](edmonton_c100_s5.html)

---

## [[Delineating Whole Cities]{.pink_font}]{text-align="center"} {.center}

---

Our method can identify a different level of clusters by choosing a different $\sigma$.

---

### Choosing $\sigma$

:::{.fragment}
- To find the optimal smoothing parameter $\sigma$, we aim to align our delineated boundaries closely with CBSAs.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- To quantify the similarity, we employ the City Jaccard (similarity) index $J_C$

$$J_C \equiv \frac{\sum_{k \in K} | U_k^{cbsa} \cap U_k^{ws}|}{|  U^{cbsa} \cup U^{ws} |}$$
where $U_k^{cbsa}$ refers to CBSA $k$ and $U_k^{ws}$ denotes the largest cluster whose center is located in CBSA $k$.

- We can measure the size of a set $|\cdot|$ using population or area size.
:::

---

### Optimal $\sigma = 11.4km$

::: {.center}
```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='80%', fig.align='center'}
include_graphics("figures/jaccard.png")
```
:::

---

### Final output (Cities)

[Continental US](https://subcenters.sanghoon.ca/)

---

Watershed cities vs. POI density

::: {.center}
<div style="width: 100%; overflow: hidden;">
  <img
    src="figures/usa_density_watershed_map.png"
    style="display: block; width: 100%; clip-path: inset(10% 0 5% 0); margin-top: -10%; margin-bottom: 0%;"
  />
</div>
:::

---

CBSAs vs. POI density

::: {.center}
<div style="width: 100%; overflow: hidden;">
  <img
    src="figures/usa_density_map.png"
    style="display: block; width: 100%; clip-path: inset(10% 0 5% 0); margin-top: -10%; margin-bottom: 0%;"
  />
</div>
:::


---

We identify the full size distribution of cities

:::{.fragment}
- A total of 1,997 cities.
  - The smallest one contains only one point of interest (POI), the median contains 371 POIs, and the largest city encompasses 271,261 POIs.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- 815 clusters matched to CBSAs (the largest in CBSAs) vs 918 CBSAs. 
  - (-) Small CBSAs are absorbed into larger nearby clusters (e.g. Trenton, NJ)
  - (+) Some CBSAs with multiple cores are divided into separate clusters (e.g. Jacksonville, FL)
:::

---

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Trenton, NJ](figures/trenton_ex.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Jacksonville, FL](figures/jacksonville_ex.png)
:::

::::

---

## [[Comparison with Density-based Clustering]{.pink_font}]{text-align="center"} {.center}

<!--
---

#### Comparison to Density-Based approach (Density distribution)

:::::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='100%'}
include_graphics("figures/atl_distribution.png")
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='100%'}
include_graphics("figures/ny_distribution.png")
```
:::
::::::

---

#### Minimum cutoff to separate Lower Manhattan and Midtown Manhattan (12.39)

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Atlanta](figures/atl_cut1239.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![New York](figures/ny_cut1239.png)
:::

::::

---

#### Maxmimum cutoff to separate Downtown, Midtown, Buckhead in Atlanta (1.5)

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Atlanta](figures/atl_cut15.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![New York](figures/ny_cut15.png)
:::

::::


---

Minimum cutoff to separate NYC and Philly is 6.9 POIs per $km^2$. Only 62 cities in the US. No cities in 15 states.
 
::: {.center}
<div style="width: 90%; margin: 0 auto; overflow: hidden;">
  <img
    src="figures/us_map69.png"
    style="display: block; width: 100%; clip-path: inset(10% 0 5% 0); margin-top: -10%; margin-bottom: 0%;"
  />
</div>
:::


---

Minimum cutoff to separate Washington D.C. and Baltimore is 11.3 POIs per $km^2$ Only 37 cities in the US. No cities in 23 states. 

::: {.center}
<div style="width: 90%; margin: 0 auto; overflow: hidden;">
  <img
    src="figures/us_map113.png"
    style="display: block; width: 100%;"
  />
</div>
:::

---

Minimum cutoff to separate San Francisco and San Jose is 13.8 POIs per $km^2$. Only 27 cities in the US. No cities in 31 states. 

::: {.center}
<div style="width: 90%; margin: 0 auto; overflow: hidden;">
  <img
    src="figures/us_map138.png"
    style="display: block; width: 100%;"
  />
</div>
:::

-->

---

### Optimal $\sigma$ and the density threshold

We tune parameters of the density-based approach to best match the CBSA boundaries.  

:::{.fragment}
- Jaccard index with Areas: $\sigma$ = 6.0$km$ threshold=0.4 POIs per $km^2$. Jaccard index = **0.137**

 Ours: $\sigma$ = 11.0$km$. Jaccard index = **0.469**.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Jaccard index with Population: $\sigma$ = 2.7$km$ threshold=1.2 POIs per $km^2$. Jaccard index = **0.695**

 Ours: $\sigma$ = 11.4$km$. Jaccard index = **0.833**.
:::

:::{.fragment}
#### Compared to the density based method

Our method produces a superior result with only one parameter.

The optimal parameter value is same both with area and population.
:::

---

Optimal density-based clusters with areas ($\sigma=6.0$ and threshold=0.4 )

::: {.center}
<div style="width: 100%; overflow: hidden;">
  <img
    src="figures/us_map_den_sig_60_cut_0.004.png"
    style="display: block; width: 100%; clip-path: inset(0% 0 3% 0); margin-top: -3%; margin-bottom: 0%;"
  />
</div>
:::


---

Optimal density-based clusters with population ($\sigma =2.7$ and threshold=1.2 )

::: {.center}
<div style="width: 100%; overflow: hidden;">
  <img
    src="figures/us_map_den_sig_27_cut_0.012.png"
    style="display: block; width: 100%; clip-path: inset(0% 0 3% 0); margin-top: -3%; margin-bottom: 0%;"
  />
</div>
:::

---

A single threshold tends to over-represent large clusters and under-represent small ones.

```{r echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/example_hor2.png")
```

---

::: {.center}
```{r echo=FALSE, out.width="75%", fig.align='center'}
include_graphics("figures/scatter_ws_density.png")
```
:::

---

::: {.center}
```{r echo=FALSE, out.width="75%", fig.align='center'}
include_graphics("figures/scatter_ws_density_zoom.png")
```
:::

 
<!--
---

## [[Other types of data]{.white_font}]{text-align="center"} {background-color="#FBBC13" .center}

---

Our algorithm can be applied to other types of data than POI data.

Raster data is straightforward.

We delineate cities with polygon data (Census 2019 CBG population).

---

### Converting polygon data to raster

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Polygon data](figures/tm_pois_atl_poly1.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Raster data](figures/tm_pois_atl_poly2.png)
:::

::::

-->

---

## [[New Facts from Subcenter and City Boundaries for US ]{.white_font}]{text-align="center"} {background-color="#FBBC13" .center}

---

## [[Central Place Theory]{.pink_font}]{text-align="center"} {.center}

---

### Hierarchy in business location patterns within cities and across cities

What business types tend to concentrate in larger subcenters (vs larger cities)?  

:::{.fragment}
**Central Place Theory**

- Higher-order services (e.g. general hospital, museum) require larger markets due to thin demand or high fixed cost and thus tend to be concentrated in larger cities. 
:::

:::{.fragment}
Verifying this pattern at the subcenter level or in small cities has been difficult, due to the lack of consistent boundary data.
:::

---

#####  POI Types (NAICS) by Avg. Cluster Size (among 4D-NAICS codes with more than 20,000 POIs)

```{r echo=FALSE}
naics_hierarchy <- read_rds("tables/avg_npois_combined.rds") %>% filter(unit=="Consumption Cluster")
top15 <- naics_hierarchy %>% arrange(desc(avg_n_pois)) %>% 
  head(15) %>% 
  mutate(avg_n_pois=round(avg_n_pois, digits=0)) %>% 
  select(`Top 15`=desc, `NAICS`=naics_code, `Avg. N of POIs`=avg_n_pois) %>% 
  knitr::kable(format = "html") %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(full_width = TRUE, font_size = 20) %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(1, width = "25em") %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(2, width = "5em") %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(3, width = "6em")

bottom15 <- naics_hierarchy %>% arrange(avg_n_pois) %>% 
  head(15) %>%   arrange(desc(avg_n_pois)) %>%
  mutate(avg_n_pois=round(avg_n_pois, digits=0)) %>% 
  select(`Bottom 15`=desc,  `NAICS`=naics_code, `Avg. N of POIs`=avg_n_pois) %>%   
  knitr::kable(format = "html") %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(full_width = TRUE, font_size = 20) %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(1, width = "25em") %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(2, width = "5em") %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(3, width = "6em")
```

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r echo=FALSE}
top15
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r echo=FALSE}
bottom15
```
:::

::::


---

#####  POI Types (NAICS) by Avg. Cluster Size at City Level (among 4D-NAICS codes with more than 20,000 POIs)

```{r echo=FALSE}

naics_hierarchy <- read_rds("tables/avg_npois_combined.rds") %>% filter(unit=="City")

top15_city <- naics_hierarchy %>% arrange(desc(avg_n_pois)) %>% 
  head(15) %>% 
  mutate(avg_n_pois=round(avg_n_pois, digits=0)) %>% 
  select(`Top 15`=desc, `NAICS`=naics_code, `Avg. N of POIs`=avg_n_pois) %>% 
  knitr::kable(format = "html") %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(full_width = TRUE, font_size = 20) %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(1, width = "25em") %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(2, width = "5em") %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(3, width = "6em")

bottom15_city <- naics_hierarchy %>% arrange(avg_n_pois) %>% 
  head(15) %>% arrange(desc(avg_n_pois)) %>%
  mutate(avg_n_pois=round(avg_n_pois, digits=0)) %>% 
  select(`Bottom 15`=desc,  `NAICS`=naics_code, `Avg. N of POIs`=avg_n_pois) %>%   
  knitr::kable(format = "html") %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(full_width = TRUE, font_size = 20) %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(1, width = "25em") %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(2, width = "5em") %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(3, width = "6em")

```

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r echo=FALSE}
top15_city
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r echo=FALSE}
bottom15_city
```
:::

::::

<!--
---

```{r echo=FALSE, out.width='60%', fig.align='center'}
include_graphics("figures/avg_npois_combined.png")
```
-->

---

### Central Place Theory is not enough to explain the hierarchy in business location patterns.

Missing land requirement as an important channel.

---

## [[Urban Wage Premium]{.pink_font}]{text-align="center"} {.center}

---

Urban Wage Premium: Average wages tend to rise with city size.


:::{.fragment}
- Does the Urban Wage Premium hold among subcenters within the same city?
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Does it persist in smaller cities?
:::

:::{.fragment}
By applying areal interpolation to Census data, we estimate average income and population for each watershed cluster.

- Areal interpolation is a spatial estimation method used to transfer data from one set of geographic boundaries (e.g., census tracts) to another (e.g., custom-defined clusters).
:::

---

#### Urban Wage Premium for Subcenters

 Subcenter-level regression

$$log(Income_{i,j}) = \beta_1 \times log(Population_i) + \delta_j + \epsilon_{i,j}$$
where $Income_i$ refers to the average income of subcenter $i$ in city $j$


:::{.fragment}
- City-level regression

$$log(Income_{j}) = \beta_1 \times log(Population_j) + \epsilon_j$$
**Note:** Regressions are weighted by population sizes. 
:::

---

#### Regression outcomes for subcenters within cities

```{r echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
table_rows <- tibble(
  ` ` = c("log(pop)", "", "N", "N of group: city", "R<sup>2</sup>"),
  `log(wage)` = c("-0.1351<sup>***</sup>", "(0.0009)", "164171", "918", "0.2847")
)

table_html <- knitr::kable(
  table_rows,
  format = "html",
  escape = FALSE,
  align = c("l", "c")
) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(full_width = FALSE)

footnote_html <- "<div style='font-size: 0.7em; margin-top: 0.4em;'>*** p&lt;0.01; ** p&lt;0.05; * p&lt;0.1</div>"

knitr::asis_output(
  paste0(as.character(table_html), footnote_html)
)
```

---

#### Urban Wage Premium for city-level regression

<table class="table" style="width: auto; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto; font-size: 0.9em;">
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th style="text-align: left;"></th>
      <th style="text-align: center;">log(wage)</th>
      <th style="text-align: center;">log(wage)</th>
      <th style="text-align: center;">log(wage)</th>
      <th style="text-align: center;">log(wage)</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td style="text-align: left;">(Intercept)</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">9.3362<sup>***</sup></td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">9.1527<sup>***</sup></td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">10.6574<sup>***</sup></td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">9.2097<sup>***</sup></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td style="text-align: left;"></td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">(0.0443)</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">(0.0725)</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">(0.1778)</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">(0.0559)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td style="text-align: left;">log(pop)</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">0.0772<sup>***</sup></td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">0.0808<sup>***</sup></td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">-0.0622<sup>***</sup></td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">0.0769<sup>***</sup></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td style="text-align: left;"></td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">(0.0031)</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">(0.0051)</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">(0.0208)</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">(0.0039)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td style="text-align: left;">Sample</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">Official CBSAs</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">Pop &ge; 10,000</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">Pop &lt; 10,000</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">All Clusters</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td style="text-align: left;">Num. obs.</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">918</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">1270</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">723</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">1993</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td style="text-align: left;">R<sup>2</sup></td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">0.4036</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">0.1663</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">0.0123</td>
      <td style="text-align: center;">0.1613</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<div style="font-size: 0.7em; margin-top: 0.4em;">*** p&lt;0.01; ** p&lt;0.05; * p&lt;0.1</div>


---

#### Urban Wage Premium for small cities (Benchmark)

```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width='40%'}
include_graphics("figures/urban_premium_official_ws.png")
```

**Watersheds with pop > 10,000:** 0.0808 (s.e. 0.0051)

**Watersheds with pop < 10,000:** -0.0622 (s.e. 0.0208)

---

#### Urban Wage Premium for small cities (all cities)

```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width='35%'}
include_graphics("figures/urban_premium_offical_ws_all_sample.png")
```

Urban Wage Premium may not work for small cities. 

**Watersheds with pop > 10,000:** 0.0808 (s.e. 0.0051)

**Watersheds with pop < 10,000:** -0.0622 (s.e. 0.0208)


---

## [[Zipf's Law]{.pink_font}]{text-align="center"} {.center}

---

### Zipf's Law for all Cities in the World

Zipf's Law: a city's size is inversely proportional to its rank.

$$S_r = \frac{S_1}{r}$$
where $S_r$ is the size of city with rank $r$ and $S_1$ is the size of the largest city. 

$$log(r) = log(S_1) - log(S_r)$$

:::{.fragment}

Three ways to test Zipf's Law:

1. Run Zipf regression and check if Zipf's coefficient $-\beta_1$ is close to 1. 
$$log(Rank_i) =\beta_0 + \beta_1 \times log(Population_i) + \epsilon_i$$
2. Examine if city size distribution follows Pareto distribution with shape parameter 1. 

3. Zipf's coefficient is stable across different minimum population cutoffs of the sample.

:::

---

## Zipf's Law for cities in the US

<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; margin-top: 1.5rem;">
  <table style="border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 0.95em; text-align: center; min-width: 520px;">
    <thead>
      <tr style="border-top: 2px solid #000; border-bottom: 1px solid #000;">
        <th style="padding: 6px 14px; text-align: left;"></th>
        <th style="padding: 6px 14px;">M1</th>
        <th style="padding: 6px 14px;">M2</th>
        <th style="padding: 6px 14px;">All</th>
      </tr>
    </thead>
    <tbody>
      <tr>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px; text-align: left;">Intercept</td>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px;">15.430<sup>***</sup></td>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px;">13.900<sup>***</sup></td>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px;">10.827<sup>***</sup></td>
      </tr>
      <tr>
        <td style="padding: 0 14px 6px; text-align: left;"></td>
        <td style="padding: 0 14px 6px;">(0.049)</td>
        <td style="padding: 0 14px 6px;">(0.037)</td>
        <td style="padding: 0 14px 6px;">(0.046)</td>
      </tr>
      <tr>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px; text-align: left;">log(Population)</td>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px;">-0.819<sup>***</sup></td>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px;">-0.698<sup>***</sup></td>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px;">-0.427<sup>***</sup></td>
      </tr>
      <tr style="border-bottom: 1px solid #000;">
        <td style="padding: 0 14px 6px; text-align: left;"></td>
        <td style="padding: 0 14px 6px;">(0.004)</td>
        <td style="padding: 0 14px 6px;">(0.003)</td>
        <td style="padding: 0 14px 6px;">(0.005)</td>
      </tr>
      <tr>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px; text-align: left;">Num. obs.</td>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px;">627</td>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px;">1270</td>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px;">1993</td>
      </tr>
      <tr style="border-bottom: 2px solid #000;">
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px; text-align: left;">R<sup>2</sup></td>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px;">0.985</td>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px;">0.972</td>
        <td style="padding: 6px 14px;">0.818</td>
      </tr>
      <tr>
        <td colspan="4" style="padding-top: 8px; text-align: left; font-size: 0.8em;">
          <sup>***</sup>p&lt;0.01; <sup>**</sup>p&lt;0.05; <sup>*</sup>p&lt;0.1
        </td>
      </tr>
    </tbody>
  </table>
</div>


---

:::: {.columns style="display: flex; align-items: center;"}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Zipf's Law Plot](figures/zipfs_all.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![City-size Distribution](figures/watershed_pop_dist.png)
:::

::::


---

## [[Delineating all cities in the world from 1975 to 2020]{.white_font}]{text-align="center"} {background-color="#FBBC13" .center}

--- 

### GHS population data

Using the Global Human Settlement (GHS) population data, we delineate cities in the world. 

:::{.fragment}
- High resolution (3 arcseconds, roughly 90m) population grids derived from satellite imagery and census data developed by the European Commission's Joint Research Centre.
:::

:::{.fragment}
We split the data into 120 UTM zones, project each zone to the corresponding UTM projection, and apply our watershed algorithm to each zone separately and then merge the outcomes.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Data spans from 1975 to 2020 in 5-year increments.
:::

---

### Delineation of all cities in the world from 1975 to 2020.

Using the official CBSA boundaries as a benchmark, we choose $\sigma= 9 km$ that our delineated boundaries closely match the CBSA boundaries, as measured by the City Jaccard index. 

Then, we apply the tuned $\sigma$ globally and delineate cities in the world from 1975 to 2020.

Using the outcomes ([Link](http://watersheds.sanghoon.ca/)), we can compare cities across countries and over time. 

---

### City Size Distribution Over Time

```{r echo=FALSE}
# 데이터 생성
tab <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Year, ~`N of Clusters`, ~Q1,     ~Median,  ~Q3,     ~Max,
  1975,  35584,            1869,    13543,    70520,   27614044,
  2000,  36628,            2291,    18159,    98046,   34193148,
  2020,  36653,            2561,    20706,    119018,  40100568
)

# gt로 표 생성
tab %>%
  gt() %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = -Year,
    decimals = 0, 
    use_seps = TRUE
  ) %>%
  cols_align(
    align = "center",
    columns = everything()
  ) %>%
  tab_options(
    table.font.size = px(40),
    table.width = pct(100)
  )
```

Global population growth has been driven mainly by the expansion of existing cities, rather than by the creation of new cities.

---

### Distribution of city sizes in the world (1975 and 2020)

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='100%', fig.align='center'}
#gereated by watersheds_ghs/tasks/urbanization.R
include_graphics("figures/world_city_dist.png")
```

City size distribution is close to log normal distribution, not Pareto distribution. 

---

### City Size Dynamics - Gibrat's Law for all cities in the World

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='70%', fig.align='center'}
include_graphics("figures/city_dynamics.png")
```

Eeckhout's findings (2004) support Gibrat’s law for U.S. cities from 1990 to 2020, except for the bottom of the distribution. Our results are broadly consistent. 

<!--
---

### Zipf's regression outcome

::: {.center}
```{r, echo=FALSE}
#gereated by watersheds_ghs/tasks/urbanization.R
include_graphics("figures/zips_world.png")
```
:::
-->


<!--
---

### Zipf's Coefficient depending on population cutoff

::: {.center}
```{r, echo=FALSE}
include_graphics("figures/zips_world_cut.png")
```
:::
-->

---

**City size relative to the largest city (with pop $>$ 50M in 2020)**

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='90%', fig.align='center'}
include_graphics("figures/zipf_7520.png")
```

<span style="color:red">Red</span> line shows relative sizes predicted by Zipf's Law. 

---

### Rising Urbanization - City Size of the Median Resident

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='95%', fig.align='center'}
#generated by "watersheds_ghs/tasks/urbanization.R"
include_graphics("figures/medium_person_city.png")
```

Rank all people by their city’s population; the median person’s city size is the population of the city where that person lives.

---

### Median Resident's City Size (1975 vs 2020)

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width='80%', fig.align='center'}
#generated by "watersheds_ghs/tasks/urbanization.R"
include_graphics("figures/med_pop_75_20.png")
```


---

## [[Conclusion]{.pink_font}]{text-align="center"} {.center}

---

This paper proposes a new method to delineate clusters by utilizing the watershed segmentation algorithm.

:::{.fragment}
- Our method has theoretical background and produces similar outcomes as the flow-based methods, but does not require flow data.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- We do not have to adjust parameter by location, allowing large scale applications.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- We have only one global parameter $\sigma$. We can use the same algorithm to identify the clusters of different levels, simply adjusting the parameter.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Our method can be applied to any type of spatial data as long as we can generate a density surface, such as pixel-based data (e.g. night-light satellite images) and polygon-based data (e.g., Census data)
:::

By applying our algorithm to POI data and satelite-based population data, we delineate:
:::{.fragment}
- consumption subcenters in the continental US. First time in the literature.
:::

:::{.fragment}
- cities in the world from 1975 to 2020. 
:::

These new datasets allow us to provide new empirical facts about urban structure and dynamics.
